new.description:
Ticket has just been created. No triage has happened yet. Content,
reproduction steps, and severity may still be incomplete.

triaged.description:
Triage has confirmed the ticket is actionable: reproducible (when
applicable), within scope, and prioritized. Not yet assigned to
work.

in_progress.description: The ticket is actively being worked on by an assignee.

waiting_customer.description:
Work is paused waiting for the reporter or customer to provide
additional information, decisions, or access. The ticket remains
open and the assignee is the same.

resolved.description:
The support team declares the ticket fixed. Awaiting verification
by the reporter. This is NOT a closed state: a rejected fix sends
the ticket back to in_progress.

verified.description:
The reporter has confirmed the fix works. Ready to be closed
administratively.

closed.description:
The ticket is administratively closed. By policy, closed tickets
can be reopened if the issue recurs. This state is terminal in
the lifecycle sense but admits a documented reverse transition,
declared explicitly via re_entry_allowed.

reopen.description:
Reopen sends the ticket back to in_progress, not to triaged,
because triage has already happened and the assignee is typically
kept. The validator is expected to warn about a terminal state
with an outgoing transition; see open_points.

duplicate.description: The ticket is closed as a duplicate of another ticket. Terminal.

wont_fix.description:
The ticket is closed without resolution by an explicit policy
decision (out of scope, by design, deferred indefinitely). Terminal.

pom.description:
Lifecycle of a support or bug ticket in an application that uses POM.
A ticket starts as new, gets triaged, may be put in progress and worked
on, may be paused waiting for customer input, gets resolved by the
support team, is then verified by the reporter, and finally closed.
Tickets can be closed as duplicates or as wont_fix from earlier stages,
and a closed ticket can be reopened.

events.name: triage
events.description: Triage decision: the ticket is actionable and may be worked on.

events.name: assign
events.description: An assignee picks up the ticket and starts work.

events.name: request_info
events.description: The assignee blocks waiting for customer or reporter input.

events.name: info_received
events.description: The requested information arrived; the assignee resumes work.

events.name: resolve
events.description:
The assignee declares the ticket fixed and hands it to the reporter
for verification.

events.name: reject_fix
events.description:
The reporter says the fix does not work; the ticket returns to
in_progress.

events.name: close
events.description:
The ticket is administratively closed after verification or after
a duplicate / wont_fix decision.

events.name: reopen
events.description:
A closed ticket is reopened because the issue recurred or new
evidence emerged.

events.name: mark_duplicate
events.description:
The ticket is identified as a duplicate of another and closed
as such.

events.name: mark_wont_fix
events.description:
The ticket is closed without implementation by an explicit
policy decision.

guards.name: has_reproduction_steps
guards.description:
The ticket has enough information to be reproduced (or, for
non-defect tickets, enough context to be actioned). Required to
pass triage.

invariants:
A ticket cannot be marked duplicate or wont_fix once it has reached
resolved or verified. Those terminal flavors apply to the early
stages of the lifecycle. After resolution, the path is verify +
close, with reopen as the only re-entry.

verified can only be reached from resolved. The reporter cannot
verify a ticket that has not been declared resolved by the
assignee.

waiting_customer never bypasses verification: from waiting_customer
the only way to terminal states is via in_progress -> resolved ->
verified -> closed. If the customer never responds, an external
policy may force mark_wont_fix from in_progress, not directly
from waiting_customer.

A reopened ticket reuses the same identifier; it does not become
a new ticket. The reopen transition is the schema-level
representation of that policy.
open_points:
Pause state modeling: waiting_customer is modeled as a primary
state here, while spec-evolution excluded Deferred/Blocked/Waiting.
The two examples make the difference explicit. Open question:
should the schema let a state declare itself a pause variant
of another state, to keep the lifecycle main path readable?
Timer-based transition: a real ticket system often closes a
ticket automatically after N days in waiting_customer. The schema
does not support timer / temporal transitions in v1 (declared out
of scope). If usage shows this is needed, a future schema may add
a time_trigger field on transitions; v1 keeps it out.
Reopen target: chosen to go back to in_progress, not to triaged.
Alternative would be reopen -> triaged when the previous assignee
is no longer available. Could be a guard rather than a separate
transition.
mark_duplicate from new is allowed even without
has_reproduction_steps. This is intentional: dedup can happen
before triage. But the schema does not currently express
"guard does not apply" — it just omits the guard. If models grow
complex, a "no_guard_required: true" marker may help readability.
closed_points:
"closed admits reopen out-transition" — resolved via the schema
attribute re_entry_allowed: true. The validator no longer flags
W003 on closed. Same decision applied to spec-evolution's
"complete" state.
